Certifiably Silly  ·  A Definitive Investigation  ·  Eyes Open, Everyone

The Sinister Truth
Behind the Smiley Face

A rigorously unhinged investigation into the world's most cheerful symbol, its dark secrets, its many faces, and what it says about the people wearing it on their chests.

By Our Senior Correspondent for Suspicious Optimism  ·  Filed from an undisclosed location (a diner)  ·  Storm clouds observed throughout

It knows It has always known Why is it smiling What does it know that you don't Probably nothing. Probably. It knows It has always known Why is it smiling What does it know that you don't Probably nothing. Probably.

It is a circle. Two dots. A curve. It is, by any reasonable measure, one of the least threatening images ever committed to paper, plastic, a bumper sticker, or the back of a stranger's jacket. And yet. And yet. There is something about the Smiley Face that has always been slightly, inexplicably, wrong.

Not wrong in a way you can report. Not wrong in a way a jury would credit. Wrong in the way a bright yellow house in an otherwise normal neighborhood is wrong — cheerful past the point of explanation, smiling at something you cannot see, apparently unbothered by conditions that should, by rights, bother everyone.

This publication has investigated the matter thoroughly. What follows are our findings. We regret nothing, except perhaps that we started.

A note on sources: Our research drew upon seventeen years of staring at the Smiley Face on coffee mugs, tote bags, refrigerator magnets, and the novelty tie worn by Dave from Accounting every single Friday. We never asked Dave what the tie meant. We were afraid of the answer.

Section OneThe Question of Happiness

Let us begin with the obvious problem. The Smiley Face is happy. Aggressively, constitutionally, non-negotiably happy. It is happy in the morning. It is happy at night. It is happy on the side of an industrial cleaning product bottle and happy on the birthday card that says You're Another Year Older! which is, if you think about it for more than a moment, not something to be happy about.

This is the first red flag. No genuinely happy entity is happy all the time. Happiness, as any therapist will tell you (and as we have been told repeatedly by ours), is a variable emotional state that occurs between other, less convenient emotional states. The Smiley Face does not have other states. It has one state. It has had that state since 1963, when commercial artist Harvey Ball drew it in ten minutes and was paid forty-five dollars.

Harvey Ball was paid forty-five dollars. The Smiley Face went on to appear on approximately fifty billion objects. Harvey Ball did not trademark it. He was reportedly fine with this. He seems, if accounts are to be believed, genuinely cheerful about the whole thing. We find this suspicious.

What are the storm clouds it is smiling through? The Smiley Face has worn its expression through recessions, wars, the invention of the leaf blower, the decline of the sit-down dinner, and every single Monday morning since Lyndon Johnson was in office. Not once has it wavered. Not once has it looked even mildly concerned. This is not resilience. This is something else.

Section TwoA Field Classification of Its Many Faces

What our investigation revealed — through extensive field observation, classified documents (a Wikipedia tab), and the attached image, which someone sent to our office without a return address — is that the Smiley Face is not a single entity. It is a type. A category. A genus with multiple species, each more unsettling than the last.

We present the official taxonomy:

🟡
Honeyed Deceit
The original. The classic. Dripping sweetness, bees circling, words smooth as warm syrup. It will tell you everything is fine. Everything is not fine. It knows this. The smile does not change.
Threat Level: Extremely Relatable
🔴
Diabolic Grin
Eyes like burning coals. Teeth that exceed what one face should reasonably contain. At least it's honest. Of all the Smiley variants, this is paradoxically the most trustworthy — it has committed to its vision.
Threat Level: Do Not Approach Near Staplers
🔵
Sorrowful Soul
Weeping. Defeated. Notes on the wall behind it read Why try? Not enough. The most emotionally honest variant. Respectable in its collapse. We understand this one.
Threat Level: Call Someone You Trust
🟠
Manic Chaos
Mismatched eyes. Paint everywhere. HA HA HA in multiple fonts. Reality: optional. Has done something — we do not know what — and found it hilarious. This is the Smiley Face at its most ungovernable.
Threat Level: Do Not Make Eye Contact
⚪
Eerie Tranquility
Calm. Still. A single candle. Everything about this one says it has already decided. It is not worried. That is exactly the problem. The most dangerous Smiley. Possibly running things from somewhere.
Threat Level: We May All Work For This One

Which face do you trust? Which one is watching you back? These are, our investigation concludes, the same face. They always were. The circle, the dots, and the curve contain multitudes, and the multitudes are not entirely pleased with you.

Section ThreeWhat It Is Hiding

The great philosophical question — posed by theologians, philosophers, and people who stare at novelty mugs too long — is not whether the Smiley Face is hiding something. It is clearly hiding something. The question is what.

Our working theories, ranked by plausibility:

Theory A: It has heard the news. All of the news. Every single piece of it, simultaneously, since 1963. The Smiley Face has been present on televisions, newspapers, refrigerators, and the internet and has processed the full uninterrupted broadcast of human events for sixty years. The smile has not changed. This either means it has achieved genuine enlightenment or its face is simply stuck and we are reading too much into it. Both possibilities are troubling.

Theory B: It is waiting. For what? Unknown. For when? Also unknown. The Smiley Face is famously patient. It waited on a warehouse wall in Worcester, Massachusetts, from 1963 until someone noticed. It will continue to wait. The smile is not joy. The smile is patience.

Theory C: It finds us funny. Not in a kind way. Not in an oh you charming humans and your little foibles way. In a way that makes the slight upward curve of that mouth somewhat more ominous in hindsight. We're the bit. We've always been the bit.

Section FourWhat Wearing One on a T-Shirt Reveals About You

We must now address the human vector. The people who, of their own free will, look at the Smiley Face and think: yes. This is something I want on my body. I will pay for this. I will wear it in public where others will see it.

This is a population we have studied at length. The findings are below.

The Ironic Wearer
Wears the Smiley Face to communicate that they are too sophisticated to wear the Smiley Face sincerely, and yet here it is, on their chest, at a brunch. The Smiley Face does not understand irony. It is just happy you're wearing it.
Hidden meaning: "I have read too much Camus"
The Vintage Seeker
Found it at a thrift store. Paid four dollars. Feels good about this. The shirt is from 1992 and has been worn by at least three previous people, each of whom had their own complicated relationship with optimism.
Hidden meaning: "I'm approachable but interesting"
The Sincere Enjoyer
Just… likes it. Finds it cheery. Has no second thoughts about this. Sleeps well. Our most studied subject. Our most envied subject. Science cannot explain how they do it.
Hidden meaning: Possibly no hidden meaning. Terrifying.
The One Who Knows
Has looked at the Smiley Face too long. Has read this article, or something like it. Wears the shirt anyway. Possibly as a warning. Possibly as an alliance signal. We are not sure whose side they are on.
Hidden meaning: There is no hidden meaning. That's the hidden meaning.

Section FiveShould We Be Worried?

Yes.

No.

The answer is both, simultaneously, which is an answer the Smiley Face would understand intuitively, because it has been yes and no at the same time for sixty years and the experience has not changed its expression in the slightest.

Here is what we know: the Smiley Face is everywhere. It is on your coffee cup and your email signature and the button your coworker pinned to their lanyard and the side of the van that was parked outside your house for six hours last Tuesday. It is on products that make you happy and products that should make no one happy and promotional materials for events you did not know were happening. It is, in some documented cases, on the face of the moon if you look at it slightly wrong at a certain angle during certain atmospheric conditions.

The Smiley Face does not need your worry. It does not need your concern, your suspicion, your investigative journalism, or your therapy bills. It will simply continue to smile. It was smiling before you got here. It will be smiling after you leave. This is perhaps the most reassuring thing about it, and also the least.

We asked a philosophy professor what it all means. She said the Smiley Face represents the human desire to impose cheerfulness on a fundamentally neutral universe. Then she looked at our notes for a long moment and said, "You're going to be all right." She did not sound certain. She smiled, though. We noticed that.

Final SectionOur Official Conclusion

The Verdict of This Investigation

The Smiley Face is real. It is also a mask. It is hiding everything and nothing simultaneously, which is, when you think about it, what most faces are doing most of the time. It is happy because happiness is the only setting it was given and it has chosen to make the most of it, which is, frankly, more than can be said for many of us.

Is it watching? Probably not. Is it judging? Almost certainly. Has it already made its peace with the situation, the weather, the state of things, and the fact that it ended up on a bumper sticker that has been partially scratched off and reads only ☺ AVE A NI? Yes. It has. It smiles on.

The Smiley Face is fine. We are the ones who are not entirely fine. The Smiley Face has known this for years and has found it hilarious.

☻   ☻   ☻
This investigation was conducted under the auspices of the Institute for Objects That Are Probably Fine  ·  No Smiley Faces were disturbed during the writing of this article  ·  One was observed the entire time  ·  It did not blink  ·  It does not have eyelids  ·  We are aware of this  ·  That does not make it better
